retrieved_docs.title: Autorefractors -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: The patient should be explained to keep the arms on the table, chin on the chinrest, and head should rest against the forehead rest. Then the chin adjustment should be made to align the visual axis. The height can be moved up and down with a height adjustment knob. Each step should be explained to the patient while operating the autorefractor.

retrieved_docs.title: Vagal Maneuver -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Diving Reflex - The adult patient should be in a comfortable seated position for 1 to 3 minutes. They should take multiple deep breaths, then hold their breath in inspiration. Immediately, the patient immerses their face into the basin of water. The physician should speak to them throughout this time in a calming voice, informing them about the progress of the procedure. The patient may stay immersed as long as tolerated but should resurface if they become air hungry. [9] For the infant or young pediatric patient, place the patient in a supine position, and cover the forehead and nose with the bag of ice water for up to 30 seconds. Observe for resolution of the SVT.

retrieved_docs.title: Deep Plane Facelift -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: A deep plane facelift aims to restore a more youthful and rested appearance to the aging face. This activity reviews the evaluation and management of patients undergoing the deep plane facelift and explains the role of the interprofessional team in improving care for patients who undergo this procedure.

retrieved_docs.title: Facial Nerve Trauma -- Treatment / Management -- Mid and Lower Face
retrieved_docs.content: While elevating the oral commissure to correct a droop in the corner of the mouth can be readily accomplished with static reanimation techniques, providing movement to the smile on the paralyzed side of the face is substantially more complicated and requires precise control over the vector and amount of oral commissure movement as well as its timing. Dynamic reanimation of the smile can be performed using either regional or free muscle transfer, with the most common muscles used being the temporalis (as a regional flap) and the gracilis (as a free muscle flap). Regional muscle transfer is less time-consuming than free muscle transfer and is technically easier to perform. The primary drawback to regional muscle transfer is that the amount of oral commissure movement produced is not as great as that achieved with free muscle transfer, and there are limitations to the vectors of movement that can be obtained as well. As an example, masseteric muscle transfer is a technique that involves releasing a portion of the inferior aspect of the masseter and suturing it to the oral commissure so that a smile would occur when the patient clenches the jaw because the origin of the masseter muscle is the zygomatic arch, which lies too far posteriorly to the oral commissure, the vector of movement during the smile produced by masseter muscle transfer is much more lateral than that of a natural smile and the technique has been largely abandoned, except some surgeons who reposition the origin of the muscle more anteriorly to achieve the correct vector. [70]

retrieved_docs.title: Rotation Flaps -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: The vector of tension for a rotation flap is directed perpendicularly along the arc of the incision, which, in a classic rotation flap, is perpendicular to the tension vector for primary wound closure. Like most flaps, the area of greatest tension is at the closure of the secondary defect. For a pure rotation flap, the maximal tension is directed perpendicular to the arc of rotation, 90 to 135 degrees away from the defect. [7] If there is an advancement component to the flap, the point of maximal tension will be at the tip of the rotation flap. For larger defects, the length and curve of the incision required for flap transfer may run perpendicular to relaxed skin tension lines or cross into other cosmetic units, which can be a limiting factor for the use of rotational flaps. However, the length of the incision may be used advantageously to recruit distant tissue or to hide standing cone deformities in less aesthetically sensitive locations. A good example of a rotational flap used for aesthetic purposes is face lifting, which employs a large rotation-advancement flap to resuspend and smooth the soft tissues of the face.

retrieved_docs.title: Z-Plasty -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Z-plasty is a commonly employed transposition flap utilized in plastic and reconstructive surgery to revise scars. [1] [2] The technique has been applied to numerous areas of the body, from fingers to the nose, the chest to the palate, the face, the eye, the ear, and many others besides. [3] [4] [5] [6] [7] [8] [9] [10] Previously referred to as “converging triangular flaps,” Z-plasty involves the transposition of two or more opposing flaps raised along a shared axis. A benefit of this procedure over other scar revision techniques is that it does not necessarily require skin excision if the quality of the skin overlying the scar is aesthetically acceptable for use in the reconstruction. Z-plasty transposition changes the direction of a scar, so it is more easily hidden within a border between facial regions or relaxed skin tension lines (RSTLs). Additionally, this technique may be employed to release scar contracture after burns. [11] Common variants of the basic Z-plasty include the planimetric Z-plasty, double-opposing Z-plasty, compound Z-plasty, skew Z-plasty, and running/serial Z-plasty.

retrieved_docs.title: Direct Brow Lift -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: The patient should undergo a preoperative evaluation by a medical professional to assess surgical risk and optimize for general anesthesia, if required. A thorough physical examination should be conducted with the patient in an upright position, focusing on brow position, visual fields, rhytids, scars, and facial nerve function. To accurately assess brow position at rest, the patient should be instructed to close their eyes and fully relax the forehead, as many individuals with significant brow ptosis involuntarily contract the frontalis muscle when their eyes are open.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Counterstrain and Facilitated Positional Release (FPR) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: FPR is similar to SCS in that the physician places the patient in a position of comfort after identifying a sore area. However, an additional activating force is applied to relax the affected muscle faster. Stanley Schiowitz developed this technique in 1990. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthetic Considerations In Bariatric Surgery -- Issues of Concern -- Intraoperative
retrieved_docs.content: A common misconception is that the patient's adipose tissue is an adequate cushion and prevents pressure injuries. The body habitus can obscure positioning mistakes and leave the patient vulnerable to entrapment injury, nerve damage, and rhabdomyolysis. Positioning devices may include a footboard or stirrups in the steep Trendelenburg or modified Lloyd–Davies positions. The extremities should be secured and padded in a neutral position. Shifting, compression, or overstretching can cause injury (eg, sprained or fractured ankles, sciatic or brachial plexus injury). Positioning can be done before induction of anesthesia to allow the patient to aid in positioning for comfort and safety. Muscle paralysis during anesthesia often masks painful positions that patients are not flexible enough to accommodate awake. Repeated checks during the procedure should be done to ensure they remain free from entrapment and undue pressure or stretch. Pre-induction airway preparation includes the patient being ramped with positioning cushions or blankets to provide optimal airway visualization with or without video laryngoscopy. These devices are removed after intubation to prevent brachial plexus stretch injuries. Alternatively, the patient bed may be placed in an approximately 25- to 40-degree reverse Trendelenburg position to decrease the compression of the lung structures and improve ventilatory mechanics as much as possible during preoxygenation when the patient is spontaneously breathing. Caution should be used when relying on reverse Trendelenburg alone to prepare for intubation, as this position may not compensate for other difficulties in the patient's airway.

retrieved_docs.title: Rhytidectomy -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: Some surgeons may place drains and a pressure dressing with ice packs; others may avoid drains and consider using a fibrin tissue sealant to reduce dead space under the facial flaps. If other procedures, such as blepharoplasty, brow lift, fat transfer, or laser resurfacing, are to be undertaken during the same anesthetic, additional equipment will be required.

retrieved_docs.title: Deep Plane Facelift -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the indications and contraindications of the deep plane facelift. Describe the equipment, personnel, preparation, and technique needed to perform the deep plane facelift. Review the evaluation of the potential complications of the deep plane facelift. Explain interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to advance the deep plane facelift and improve outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2132
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical Manipulation is a broad category of operative techniques that encompasses several specific procedures. Among these, the Application of therapeutic agent refers to the intra‑operative delivery of medication or biologic substances to treat disease directly at the surgical site. The Delivery of vertex presentation describes an obstetric maneuver used during childbirth to assist the passage of a fetus presenting head‑first, often requiring careful manual handling to ensure safe delivery. Manual conversion of position denotes a technique in which a clinician manually repositions a patient’s body part or the entire patient to achieve optimal anatomical alignment for subsequent treatment or to correct malposition. Together, these three child procedures illustrate the diverse ways in which surgical manipulation can be employed to administer therapy, facilitate delivery, and adjust anatomical positioning, all of which are integral to effective patient care.

Recognizing this presentation is important for obstetric assessment and delivery planning, as it influences the choice of birth method and the management of potential complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2732
retrieved_docs.content: Application of therapeutic agent, Combined obstetrical version, and Manual conversion of position are all specific types of surgical manipulation. As child procedures of the broader category of surgical manipulation, each involves an operative technique aimed at altering a physiological condition during a medical intervention. Application of therapeutic agent refers to the intra‑operative delivery of medication to achieve a therapeutic effect directly at the target site. Combined obstetrical version denotes a coordinated obstetric maneuver performed surgically to reposition a fetus to a more favorable presentation for delivery. Manual conversion of position describes a hands‑on technique used during surgery to adjust the patient’s or organ’s orientation to improve access or outcomes. Recognizing these procedures as subcategories of surgical manipulation helps clinicians select appropriate operative strategies, anticipate procedural requirements, and optimize patient care through targeted therapeutic and positional interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2056
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical Manipulation is a broad category of operative interventions that includes specific techniques such as Application of Therapeutic Agent and Manual Conversion of Position. Application of Therapeutic Agent refers to the intra‑operative delivery of medication or other therapeutic substances, while Manual Conversion of Position involves the physical repositioning of tissues, organs, or skeletal structures during a procedure. Both Application of Therapeutic Agent and Manual Conversion of Position are considered child concepts within Surgical Manipulation, illustrating the diverse range of actions that fall under this overarching surgical domain.
